blocks.content: In this milestone, you translate the raw summary into benchmark numbers you will encode as thresholds next.
You'll capture p95 latency, error rate, and throughput, then decide how much headroom to allow before a threshold fails.

Find **`http_req_duration`** and note **`p(95)`** as your latency baseline. Example:

```text
http_req_duration..: avg=180ms  min=95ms
                     med=165ms  max=850ms
                     p(90)=280ms  p(95)=350ms
```

Here the p95 latency baseline is **350 ms**.

p95 is a practical starting point: it reflects most users while muting rare spikes. You can tighten to p90 or add p99 later once the process feels stable.

Find **`http_req_failed`** and note the percentage as your error-rate baseline:

```text
http_req_failed................: 0.00%   0 out of 1200
```

Zero is ideal; thresholds usually keep a small tolerance (for example below 1%) for transient failures.

Find **`iterations`** and note the per-second rate as throughput at your chosen load:

```text
iterations.....................: 1200    10.00/s
```

Here throughput is about **10 iterations per second** at 20 virtual users.

Write the numbers down in a simple table before editing thresholds:

| Metric | Baseline value | Example threshold |
| --- | --- | --- |
| p95 latency | your observed p95 | add headroom (see below) |
| Error rate | your observed rate | `rate<0.01` (allow under 1%) |
| Throughput | your observed RPS | monitor, optional to threshold |

Headroom means setting the latency threshold higher than the observed p95 so normal variance does not fail the test. A common approach is to multiply observed p95 by **1.2 to 1.5** (20% to 50% above). Example: p95 53 ms times 1.3 rounds to **`p(95)<70`**. Use smaller multipliers for very stable services and larger multipliers when variance is high.
You now have explicit baseline figures and a headroom rule you can copy into `thresholds`.
In the next milestone, you encode those values as k6 thresholds.
